---
title: Third Tutorial for R4DS
author: Luke Li, David Kane, and Sruthi Gandhi
tutorial:
  id: r4ds-3
output:
  learnr::tutorial:
    df_print: default
    progressive: true
    allow_skip: true
runtime: shiny_prerendered
description: 'This tutorial for "R for Data Science" covers Chapter 12: Logical vectors, Chapter 13: Numbers, Chapter 14: Strings, Chapter 15: Regular expressions, and Chapter 21: Databases. Exercises practice stringr operations, parse_number, and querying data with DBI and dbplyr.'
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(learnr)
library(tutorial.helpers)

library(tidyverse)
library(babynames)
library(nycflights13)
library(DBI)
library(duckdb)
library(dbplyr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(out.width = '90%')
options(tutorial.exercise.timelimit = 600,
        tutorial.storage = "local")

con_names <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(),
                       dbdir = "data/nameby_year.duckdb")
con_air <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(),
                     dbdir = "data/nycflights13.duckdb")

dbplyr_names <- tbl(con_names, "babynames")
dbplyr_fly <- tbl(con_air, "flights")

x <- dbplyr_names |>
  filter(str_starts(name, "A"), year > 2000, n >= 10) |>
  mutate(length_class = if_else(str_length(name) <= 5, "Short", "Long")) |>
  summarize(total = sum(n), .by = c(year, length_class)) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  collect()

departure_data <- flights |>
  select(dep_time, sched_dep_time, dep_delay, tailnum) |>
  mutate(tail_number = parse_number(tailnum)) |>
  filter(!is.na(dep_time)) |>
  mutate(
    dep_hour = dep_time %/% 100,
    dep_minute = dep_time %% 100,
    sched_hour = sched_dep_time %/% 100,
    sched_minute = sched_dep_time %% 100,
    true_dep_delay = pmax(dep_delay, 0)
  ) |>
  count(sched_hour, dep_hour, wt = true_dep_delay, name = "total_delay")
```

```{r info-section, child = system.file("child_documents/info_section.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```

## Introduction
###

This section covers key concepts from [Chapter 13: Numbers](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/numbers.html), [Chapter 14: Strings](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/strings.html), [Chapter 15: Regular Expressions](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/regexps.html), [Chapter 12: Logical Vectors](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/logicals.html), and [Chapter 21: Databases](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/databases.html) from [*R for Data Science (2e)*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund.

You will learn about working with numeric data and text patterns using core *Tidyverse* packages like **readr**, **dplyr**, and **stringr**, as well as database interaction using **dbplyr**. Key concepts include extracting numbers from strings, detecting text patterns, computing parallel maxima across vectors, and connecting to databases.

We recommend using an in-environment AI agent such as [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) or [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code); instructions are written with these tools in mind. You may also use a chat-based AI, but you will need to copy/paste code and data context manually.

### Exercise 1

Create a new repo using `codespace-starter` as a template and name it `r4ds-3`.

If you haven't already, open the `r4ds-3` repo on GitHub and create a Codespace from it.

You need two R Terminals: one for running this tutorial and one for your exercises. Open a second R Terminal using the dropdown button.

Create a new file and save it as `analysis.qmd`. Add a YAML header at the top with a title (`"Analyzing Baby Names and Flights"`) and your name as author. In a bash terminal, run:

```
quarto render analysis.qmd
```

Open `analysis.html` in a browser by right-clicking it in the File Explorer and selecting **Open with Live Server**. Keep this tab open — it will refresh automatically whenever you re-render.

Create a `.gitignore` file with `analysis_files` on the first line and then a blank line. Save and push.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file(".gitignore")
```

If that fails, it is probably because you have not yet loaded `library(tutorial.helpers)` in the R Terminal.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-1}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 3)
```

###

We will be working with databases in this tutorial. Two key differences between data frames and database tables: database tables are stored on disk and can be arbitrarily large, while data frames are stored in memory; database tables almost always have indexes for fast row lookups, while data frames do not.

### Exercise 2

In your QMD, put `library(tidyverse)`, `library(DBI)`, `library(duckdb)`, and `library(dbplyr)` in a new code chunk. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab — it should refresh automatically.

Notice that the file does not look good because the code is visible and there are annoying messages. To take care of this, add `#| message: false` to remove all the messages in this code chunk. Also add the following to the YAML header to remove all code echoes from the HTML:

```
execute:
  echo: false
```

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab. In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-2}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 6)
```

###

Databases are run by database management systems (DBMS's). DuckDB is an in-process DBMS — it runs entirely within your R session, requires no separate server, and is ideal for analyzing large local datasets.

### Exercise 3

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Confirm that the Live Server tab auto-refreshes.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-3}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 3)
```

###

At the simplest level, you can think of a database as a collection of data frames, called tables in database terminology. Like a data frame, a database table is a collection of named columns where every value in a column is the same type.

### Exercise 4

From the R Terminal, run these three commands:

`getwd()`
`dir.create("data")`
`list.files()`

This will create a `data` directory in your project.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

Your answer should look something like this, although your path will be different.

````
> getwd()
[1] "/workspaces/r4ds-3"
> dir.create("data")
> list.files()
 [1] "analysis_files" "analysis.html"  "analysis.qmd"   "data"
>
````


## Baby names
###

In this section we use a DuckDB database called `nameby_year.duckdb`. The data comes from the **babynames** package, which contains names used for American babies from 1880 to 2017.

Key concepts you'll use: `DBI::dbConnect()` to open a database connection, `tbl()` to create a lazy table reference, and `collect()` to pull results into R.

```{r babynames-data}
dbplyr_names
```

### Exercise 1

We begin by downloading a DuckDB version of the babynames dataset directly from GitHub using `download.file()`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
download.file(
  "https://github.com/PPBDS/vscode.tutorials/raw/refs/heads/main/inst/tutorials/r4ds-3/data/nameby_year.duckdb",
  destfile = "data/nameby_year.duckdb",
  mode = "wb"
)
```

CP/CR.

```{r baby-names-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

`download.file()` saves a file from a URL to a local path. The `mode = "wb"` argument is required for binary files like `.duckdb` — without it, text-mode line-ending conversion can corrupt the file.

### Exercise 2

Ask AI to connect to `data/nameby_year.duckdb` using `DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = ...)` and assign the result to `con_names`. Then create a lazy table reference to the `"babynames"` table using `tbl(con_names, "babynames")` and assign it to `dbplyr_names`. Add both lines to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r baby-names-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

```{r baby-names-2-test}
dbplyr_names
```

`DBI::dbConnect()` creates a connection to a database. The first argument selects the DBMS (`duckdb::duckdb()`), and subsequent arguments describe where to find it. **DBI** handles the low-level SQL execution; **dbplyr** translates your **dplyr** code into SQL queries that DBI then runs.

### Exercise 3

Use the following prompt with your AI to build a summary of baby names starting with "A". Add the resulting code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

Here's the prompt we used:

Write R code that starts with `dbplyr_names` and pipes it through: filter to keep rows where the name starts with 'A' using `str_starts(name, 'A')`, where `year > 2000`, and where `n >= 10`; mutate to add a `length_class` column that is 'Short' if `str_length(name) <= 5` or 'Long' otherwise using `if_else()`; summarize to compute the total count of babies per year and length class using `sum(n)` with `.by = c(year, length_class)`, naming the result `total`; arrange by `year`; and collect the results into R using `collect()`.

```{r baby-names-3}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 6)
```

###

Our result:

```{r baby-names-3-test}
x
```

If your result doesn't match ours, replace your chunk code with the following before moving on:

<pre><code>```{r}
dbplyr_names |>
  filter(str_starts(name, "A"), year > 2000, n >= 10) |>
  mutate(length_class = if_else(str_length(name) <= 5, "Short", "Long")) |>
  summarize(total = sum(n), .by = c(year, length_class)) |>
  arrange(year) |>
  collect()
```</code></pre>

`tbl()` creates a lazy table — operations on it are translated to SQL and executed in the database, not in R's memory. Results are only retrieved into R when you call `collect()`. `str_starts()` and `str_length()` from **stringr** translate automatically to their SQL equivalents in a **dbplyr** pipeline.

### Exercise 4

Within the latest code chunk, add the option: `#| cache: true`. Assign the result of the pipe to `x`. If you have not done so already, add `analysis_cache` to your `.gitignore`.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. This first render will take some time — by including `#| cache: true`, Quarto saves the result to disk so the next render loads from cache instead of re-running. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r baby-names-4}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 8)
```

###

Everything before `collect()` in a **dbplyr** pipeline runs as SQL in the database. Once you call `collect()`, the results transfer into R as a tibble. The `.by` argument in `summarize()` performs grouped operations without creating a persistent grouped data frame, unlike `group_by()`.

### Exercise 5

Ask AI to pipe `x` into a line graph showing the proportion of "Short" vs. "Long" names starting with "A" over the years, with `length_class` mapped to `color`. Within `labs()`, add a proper title, subtitle, and caption. Don't assign the plot. Add this code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r baby-names-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 12)
```

###

```{r baby-names-5-test}
ggplot(x, aes(x = year, y = total, color = length_class)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Trends in Short vs Long A-Names Since 2000",
    subtitle = "Short baby names starting with A are becoming more popular",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Babies",
    color = "Name Length",
    caption = "Source: babynames"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  theme_minimal()
```

Creating categorical variables like `length_class` transforms continuous data (name length in characters) into discrete groups that are easier to interpret and visualize, revealing broader patterns that individual values might obscure.

## Flights
###

This section covers key concepts from [Chapter 13: Numbers](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/numbers.html) and [Chapter 15: Regular Expressions](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/regexps.html). The `nycflights13` dataset contains information about all flights departing from New York City airports in 2013.

Key functions you'll use: `parse_number()` to extract numeric values from strings, `%/%` and `%%` for integer division and modulo, and `pmax()` to compute element-wise maximums.

```{r flights-data}
dbplyr_fly
```

### Exercise 1

We begin by downloading a DuckDB version of the flights dataset directly from GitHub using `download.file()`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
download.file(
  "https://github.com/PPBDS/vscode.tutorials/raw/refs/heads/main/inst/tutorials/r4ds-3/data/nycflights13.duckdb",
  destfile = "data/nycflights13.duckdb",
  mode = "wb"
)
```

CP/CR.

```{r flights-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

The `nycflights13.duckdb` database contains multiple tables: `airlines`, `airports`, `flights`, `planes`, and `weather`. Use `dbListTables(con_air)` to list them after connecting.

### Exercise 2

Ask AI to connect to `data/nycflights13.duckdb` using `DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = ...)` and assign the result to `con_air`. Then create a lazy table reference to the `"flights"` table using `tbl(con_air, "flights")` and assign it to `dbplyr_fly`. Add both lines to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r flights-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

```{r flights-2-test}
dbplyr_fly
```

Lazy tables let you explore database structure without loading data into memory. When you print a lazy table, **dbplyr** shows column names, types, and a preview — all from a lightweight SQL query. This is crucial for understanding unfamiliar databases before writing complex queries.

### Exercise 3

Use the following prompt with your AI to summarize departure delay patterns. Add the resulting code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

Here's the prompt we used:

Write R code that starts with `dbplyr_fly` and pipes it through: select only `dep_time`, `sched_dep_time`, `dep_delay`, and `tailnum`; mutate to add a `tail_number` column by extracting numbers from `tailnum` using `parse_number()`; filter to remove rows where `dep_time` is missing using `!is.na(dep_time)`; collect the results into R; then mutate to add `dep_hour` as `dep_time %/% 100`, `dep_minute` as `dep_time %% 100`, `sched_hour` as `sched_dep_time %/% 100`, `sched_minute` as `sched_dep_time %% 100`, and `true_dep_delay` as `pmax(dep_delay, 0)` to floor negative delays at zero; and finally count by `sched_hour` and `dep_hour`, weighting by `true_dep_delay`, naming the result column `total_delay`.

```{r flights-3}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 6)
```

###

Our result:

```{r flights-3-test}
departure_data
```

If your result doesn't match ours, replace your chunk code with the following before moving on:

<pre><code>```{r}
dbplyr_fly |>
  select(dep_time, sched_dep_time, dep_delay, tailnum) |>
  mutate(tail_number = parse_number(tailnum)) |>
  filter(!is.na(dep_time)) |>
  collect() |>
  mutate(
    dep_hour = dep_time %/% 100,
    dep_minute = dep_time %% 100,
    sched_hour = sched_dep_time %/% 100,
    sched_minute = sched_dep_time %% 100,
    true_dep_delay = pmax(dep_delay, 0)
  ) |>
  count(sched_hour, dep_hour, wt = true_dep_delay, name = "total_delay")
```</code></pre>

`%/%` is integer division (e.g., `517 %/% 100 = 5`) and `%%` is modulo (e.g., `517 %% 100 = 17`). Together they extract hours and minutes from four-digit military time. `pmax()` computes the element-wise maximum across vectors — `pmax(dep_delay, 0)` floors each delay at zero, preventing early departures from offsetting late ones.

### Exercise 4

Within the latest code chunk, add the option: `#| cache: true`. Assign the result of the pipe to `departure_data`. If you have not done so already, add `analysis_cache` to your `.gitignore`.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. This first render will take some time. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r flights-4}
question_text(NULL,
    answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
    allow_retry = TRUE,
    try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
    incorrect = NULL,
    rows = 8)
```

###

Weighted counts with `count(wt = ...)` transform a frequency table into a sum table. Instead of counting how many flights fall into each scheduled/actual hour combination, we're summing the total delay minutes — revealing which time combinations contribute most to overall delays.

### Exercise 5

Ask AI to pipe `departure_data` into a heatmap with `sched_hour` on the x-axis, `dep_hour` on the y-axis, and `total_delay` as the fill color. Within `labs()`, add a proper title, subtitle, and caption. Don't assign the plot. Add this code to a new code chunk in your QMD. In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r flights-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 12)
```

###

```{r flights-5-test}
ggplot(departure_data, aes(x = sched_hour, y = dep_hour, fill = total_delay)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Total Delay\n(minutes)") +
  labs(
    title = "Flight Delay Patterns: Scheduled vs Actual Departure Hours",
    subtitle = "Darker regions show higher total delay concentrations",
    x = "Scheduled Departure Hour",
    y = "Actual Departure Hour",
    caption = "Source: nycflights13"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12)
  )
```

Once you map an aesthetic, **ggplot2** takes care of the rest. It selects a reasonable scale and constructs a legend that explains the mapping. For heatmaps, `scale_fill_viridis_c()` provides a perceptually uniform color scale that works well for continuous data.

## Summary

###

This tutorial covered [Chapter 12: Logical vectors](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/logicals.html), [Chapter 13: Numbers](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/numbers.html), [Chapter 14: Strings](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/strings.html), [Chapter 15: Regular expressions](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/regexps.html), and [Chapter 21: Databases](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/databases.html) from [*R for Data Science (2e)*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund.

You learned how to work with numbers, strings, and logical vectors using **readr**, **stringr**, and **dplyr**, and how to query databases using **DBI** and **dbplyr**.

### Exercise 1

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd` to ensure that everything works. Check your Live Server tab — the resulting HTML page should be attractive, showing clean versions of your plots.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd")
```

CP/CR.

```{r summary-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 30)
```

###

Database operations with **dbplyr** are translated to SQL and executed in the database, with results only pulled into R when you call `collect()`.

### Exercise 2

Publish your rendered QMD to GitHub Pages. In a bash terminal, run:

````
quarto publish gh-pages analysis.qmd
````

If the bash terminal is still running from rendering, stop it first using `Ctrl/Cmd + C`.

Copy/paste the resulting URL below.

```{r summary-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 1)
```

###

Publishing the QMD creates a public URL anyone can use to view the rendered project.

### Exercise 3

Commit and push all your files. Copy/paste the URL to your GitHub repo.

```{r summary-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

GitHub repositories are where people can view the code you used to create your project.

```{r download-answers, child = system.file("child_documents/download_answers.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```
